title: Cat Excessive Drooling and Pawing at Mouth: Causes & When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Is your cat drooling and pawing at their face? Learn about common causes, urgency levels, and why a photo can help your vet triage the situation.
content_html:
TL;DR: Excessive drooling and mouth pawing in cats indicate significant oral pain or obstruction and require a veterinary examination within 24 hours.

Why is my cat drooling and pawing at their mouth?

  • Excessive drooling (ptyalism) combined with mouth pawing is a clear sign of oral discomfort, pain, or a physical obstruction.
  • This behavior often suggests internal issues such as dental disease, a foreign object stuck in the gums, or a reaction to a chemical irritant or toxin.

How urgent is it if my cat is drooling and pawing at their mouth?

  • The urgency level for this condition is categorized as Medium, indicating significant pain or distress that requires attention.
  • Contact your veterinarian to schedule an exam within the next 24 hours.
  • Seek immediate care if you notice facial swelling, an inability to swallow, or lethargy.

How can photos and videos of my cat’s mouth help the vet?

  • A clear photo or short video helps the veterinary team distinguish between nausea and physical pain.
  • Capturing the drool and the specific way your cat is pawing can reveal visible foreign objects like string or splinters.
  • Visual documentation allows the clinic to prepare for specific procedures before you even arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Excessive drooling (ptyalism) and pawing at the mouth in cats can be associated with oral inflammation such as stomatitis or glossitis. Halitosis and blood-tinged saliva may also be present. Affected animals may resent oral examination due to pain, and regional lymph nodes may be enlarged. Potential causes include oral foreign bodies, neoplasms, injuries, infectious diseases (e.g., rabies), reluctance to swallow due to esophageal or gastrointestinal issues, sublingual lesions, tonsillitis, drug administration, conformational defects, metabolic disorders (e.g., hepatic encephalopathy, uremia), and salivary gland issues. Rabies should be ruled out. Feline stomatitis (FS) is a relatively uncommon but serious cause characterized by progressively worsening inflammation of oral mucosal tissues, particularly the gingiva, alveolar, labial, buccal, and sublingual mucosa, and the mucosa of the caudal oral cavity. Severe ulceroproliferative inflammation involving the area at and lateral to the palatoglossal folds bilaterally in the back of the mouth is pathognomonic for FS.

Chapter: Dermatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 362)

faqs.question: Could my cat have something stuck in their teeth?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is very common for foreign objects like blades of grass, string, or small bone fragments to get lodged in the teeth or the roof of the mouth, causing immediate drooling and pawing.

faqs.question: Are dental diseases a likely cause?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. Conditions such as feline resorptive lesions, severe gingivitis, or an abscessed tooth are frequent culprits for sudden oral sensitivity and excessive salivation.

faqs.question: Can toxic plants cause these symptoms?
faqs.answer: Yes, many common household plants can cause oral irritation or chemical burns if chewed, leading to profuse drooling and attempts to clear the mouth with their paws.
